coredata.dc:title: Dual emission ratiometric fluorescence probe based on N-doped carbon dots and coumarin derivatives for determination of moxifloxacin and copper ion
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qihui
Yang, Ju
Yang, Siqi
Li, Jiagen
Chen, Congdi
Wang, Jiexue
Xiong, Zhihui
Sun, Kang
Deng, Guowei
coredata.dc:description:
Background Residues of moxifloxacin (MFX) and copper ions (Cu2+) can have detrimental health effects on humans. Thus, it is essential to develop a sensitive method for the detection of MFX and Cu2+. Fluorescence analysis is a potent technique, offering simplicity, speed, and efficiency. However, analytical results from single-emission fluorescence methods can be affected by environmental factors, probe inhomogeneity, and light-source intensity. Therefore, there is a need to design a novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe for the determination of MFX and Cu2+ to address these challenges and enhance the accuracy and sensitivity of the measurements.
Results A novel dual emission ratiometric-based fluorescence probe (B/G probe) was constructed by N-doped carbon dots and coumarin derivatives. The B/G probe exhibited two fluorescence emission peaks at 442 nm and 513 nm when excited at 336 nm. The inner-filter effect played a significant role in the quenching characteristics of MFX. Additionally, Cu2+ ions were found to have a strong affinity for MFX. Consequently, this fluorescence probe was developed to detect MFX initially and subsequently Cu2+ ions. The detection limit (DL) was 17.5 nM and 32.5 nM for MFX and Cu2+ ions, respectively. The B/G probe had better selectivity and anti-interference capability. Satisfactory recovery rate indicated that the B/G prob had good accuracy for detection of MFX and Cu2+ ions.
Significance This study represents the inaugural application of N-doped carbon dots and coumarin derivatives to construct a novel dual emission ratiometric-based fluorescence probe, demonstrating a marked advancement in innovation. This approach has significantly enhanced the sensitivity for the determination of MFX and Cu2+ ions. Compared with fluorescence probe of single signal, this method provides an effective strategy for detection MFX and Cu2+ with good accuracy, good selectivity, rapid detection and lower DL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual emission ratiometric-based fluorescent probe
N-doped carbon dots
Coumarin derivatives
Moxifloxacin
Copper ion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM (a, b), particle distribution (c) and XPS spectra (d) of the B-probe. The UV and FL spectrum of B-probe (e), G-probe (f) and the B/G probe (g). The FT-IR spectrum of B-probe and G-probe (h).

Optical properties and stability of dual emission fluorescent probe. FL spectra of (a) B-probe, (b) G-probe and (c) the B/G probe at various excitation wavelengths. Influences of (d) pH, (e) ionic strength, (f) NaCl and (g) solvents on the stability of the B/G probe.
(a) The FL spectrum of the B/G probe before and after the addition of MFX. Influences of (b) pH, (c) time and (d) temperature on the determination of MFX, (e) the calibration curve.
(a) The FL spectrum of the B/G probe-MFX before and after the addition of Cu2+. Influences of (b) incubating time and (c) temperature on the determination of Cu2+, (d) the calibration curve.

The selectivity of the B/G probe towards MFX (a) antibiotics (b) metal ions (c) small molecules. The interference for determination of MFX (d) antibiotics (e) small molecules (f) metal ions. The interference of the B/G probe-MFX as turn-on sensor for Cu2+ determination (g) antibiotics (h) small molecules (i) metal ions.

(a) Fluorescence excitation spectra (i), fluorescence emission spectra (ii) and UV–Vis absorption spectra of the B/G probe (iii), UV–Vis absorption spectra of MFX (iv) and UV–Vis absorption spectra of the B/G probe + MFX (v). (b) Fluorescence decay curves of the B/G probe and the B/G probe + MFX. (c) The Zeta potential and (d) the XPS spectra of the B/G probe (i), the B/G probe + MFX(ii) and the B/G probe + MFX + Cu2+(iii). (e) The fluorescence emission spectra of MFX, Cu2+ before and after added in the B/G probe.
Detection of MFX and Cu2+ in milk samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: HPLC for MFX and ICP-AES for Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Dual emission ratiometric fluorescence probe based on N-doped carbon dots and coumarin derivatives for determination of moxifloxacin and copper ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Sichuan Provincial Key Laboratory for Structural Optimization and Application of Functional Molecules, Chengdu Normal University, Chengdu, 611130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science
Sichuan Provincial Key Laboratory for Structural Optimization and Application of Functional Molecules

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Sichuan Provincial Key Laboratory for Structural Optimization and Application of Functional Molecules, Chengdu Normal University, Chengdu, 611130, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Residues of moxifloxacin (MFX) and copper ions (Cu2+) can have detrimental health effects on humans. Thus, it is essential to develop a sensitive method for the detection of MFX and Cu2+. Fluorescence analysis is a potent technique, offering simplicity, speed, and efficiency. However, analytical results from single-emission fluorescence methods can be affected by environmental factors, probe inhomogeneity, and light-source intensity. Therefore, there is a need to design a novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe for the determination of MFX and Cu2+ to address these challenges and enhance the accuracy and sensitivity of the measurements.

A novel dual emission ratiometric-based fluorescence probe (B/G probe) was constructed by N-doped carbon dots and coumarin derivatives. The B/G probe exhibited two fluorescence emission peaks at 442 nm and 513 nm when excited at 336 nm. The inner-filter effect played a significant role in the quenching characteristics of MFX. Additionally, Cu2+ ions were found to have a strong affinity for MFX. Consequently, this fluorescence probe was developed to detect MFX initially and subsequently Cu2+ ions. The detection limit (DL) was 17.5 nM and 32.5 nM for MFX and Cu2+ ions, respectively. The B/G probe had better selectivity and anti-interference capability. Satisfactory recovery rate indicated that the B/G prob had good accuracy for detection of MFX and Cu2+ ions.

This study represents the inaugural application of N-doped carbon dots and coumarin derivatives to construct a novel dual emission ratiometric-based fluorescence probe, demonstrating a marked advancement in innovation. This approach has significantly enhanced the sensitivity for the determination of MFX and Cu2+ ions. Compared with fluorescence probe of single signal, this method provides an effective strategy for detection MFX and Cu2+ with good accuracy, good selectivity, rapid detection and lower DL.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Quinolone (Qs) which contains the basic structure of 4-quinolone is an efficient antibacterial agent. It mainly against Gram-negative bacteria and Mycoplasma [1]. Moxifloxacin (MFX) introduces a methoxy group at the 8th carbon atom in molecular structure, which improves the binding ability of the drug to pathogens. Moxifloxacin has high blood concentration, long half-life, and high bioavailability which is used to the respiratory system. However, excessive moxifloxacin will remain in surface water and food from animal sources, which can lead to serious health effects such as diarrhea, liver damage, and central nervous system damage [2]. Copper ion (Cu2+) is an essential trace element for living organisms. When Cu2+ is lacking or excessive, it will lead to the development of diseases.

Nowadays, MFX can be determined by chromatographic techniques [3], electroanalytical method [4], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [5], spectrophotometry [6] and surface plasmon resonance [7] etc. The above methods possessed outstanding detection performance for the detection of MFX. However there still exist some shortcomings need to be filled, such as time-consuming sample pretreatment, absence selectivity and complicated substrate preparation [8]. On the other hand, several methods have been used for the detection of Cu2+ ions such as atomic absorption spectroscopy [9], inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry/mass spectrometry [10], atomic fluorescence spectroscopy [11], and colorimetry [12]. However, these methods often require costly equipment, extensive use of organic solvents and gases, and may lack sensitivity. Consequently, there is a need to develop a detection method for MFX and Cu2+ ions that is simple, rapid, sensitive, and selective.

The fluorescence method is a powerful tool with the advantages of simplicity, fast, good selectivity [13]. Reena K. Sajwan et al. established fluorometric “on-off” probe based on polyvinyl alcohol-derived-carbon quantum dots for moxifloxacin detection in milk and egg samples [14]. Beibei Mao et al. constructed a Ag NCs–Cu2+ sensing system based on fluorescence turn-on strategy for trace detection of quinolones [15]. Shu Pang et al. developed a fluorescence method for detection of Cu2+ and Zn2+ ions based on the zero-valent iron nanoclusters [16]. Several materials such as sulfur quantum dots [17], metal ligand frameworks [18], small-molecule organic [19], silicon nanoparticles [8], carbon dots (CDs) [20], CdTe quantum dots [21] and nanoclusters [16] have been developed as fluorescence probes. CDs were employed as high-performance fluorescent nanomaterials due to their advantages of simple synthesis, high biocompatibility, low cost, and good stability. However, most fluorescence probes, particularly those based on single-emission, demonstrate very low sensitivity, selectivity, and measurement accuracy [22]. Environmental factors, probe inhomogeneous and light-source intensity can affect the analytical outcomes of single-emission-based fluorescence methods. In contrast, a dual emission ratiometric-based fluorescence probe can effectively mitigate environmental and background interference through its inherent self-correction capability [23]. Therefore, dual emission ratiometric-based fluorescence probe can be designed to overcome the above issues, improving the measurement accuracy and sensitivity.

In this work, a dual emission ratiometric-based fluorescence probe (B/G probe) was constructed by N-doped carbon dots and coumarin derivatives. This probe features two distinct fluorescence emission peaks at 442 nm and 513 nm. It has been specifically designed for the sequential and selective quantitative detection of MFX and subsequently Cu2+. To confirm accuracy, the B/G probe was used to analyze concentration in animal derived food samples like milk with satisfactory recovery rate. The method provides an effective strategy for detection MFX and Cu2+ with good accuracy and selectivity.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, moxifloxacin, florfenicol, thiamphenicol, sulfamethoxazole, erythromycin, tetracycline, chloramphenicol, levofloxacin, norfloxacin, sulfadimidine, ciprofloxacin, oxytetracycline, chlortetracycline, glucose, fructose, tryptophane, phenylalanine, lysine, tyrosine was obtained from Adamas (Shanghai, China). MgCl2, CaCl2, Cu(NO3)2, KCl, Zn(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Ni(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, NaCl, FeCl3, ethanol, acetonitrile, acetone and methanol were purchased from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Chengdu, China). All chemicals are analytical grade.

The morphology of the N-doped CDs was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos™ F200S, Thermo Scientific). Nanoparticle size analyzer was used to determinate the size of N-doped CDs (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of coumarin derivatives was obtained by X-ray photo-electron spectrometer (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Scientific) was used to collect FT-IR spectrum. The fluorescence spectrum and ultraviolet absorption spectrum were collected by fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600 Hitachi) and ultraviolet spectrophotometer (2600, Shimadzu). The fluorescent lifetimes were determined by Hitachi F7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of dual emission fluorescent probe (B/G probe)
ce:section.ce:para: The blue fluorescent probe (N-doped CDs, B-probe) was synthesized by microwave method according to our previous work [18]. The synthetic method was explained in detail in Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) S1.1. The B-probe was dispersed in ultrapure water at 1.0 mg mL−1.

The synthetic method and route of the green fluorescent probe (coumarin derivatives, G-probe) was shown in ESI S1.2 and ESI Fig. S1 [24]. The G-probe was dispersed in anhydrous ethanol at 1.0 mg mL−1.
1.0 mg mL−1 of B-probe (50 μL) and 1.0 mg mL−1 G-probe (25 μL) were added in 4.9 mL of ethanol and distilled water (1:1) to establish a dual emission fluorescent probe (B/G probe).

For the determination of MFX, a mixture was prepared in a sample vial by adding B-probe (1 mg mL−1, 50 μL), G-probe (1 mg mL−1, 25 μL) and MFX (0.01 mol L−1, 50 μL). The volume was then adjusted to 5 mL with a solvent mixture of ethanol and distilled water in a 1:1 ratio. The FL intensity was recorded at 442 nm and 513 nm with photomultiplier tube voltage of 550 V at room temperature. The ratio value of FL intensity at 513 nm and 442 nm was used to determine MFX. The absence of MFX assigned as (F513/F442)0 and the presence of MFX assigned as (F513/F442). The pH value, temperature and incubating time were optimized, and each parameter was measured three times in parallel.

For the determination of Cu2+, a mixture was prepared in a sample vial by adding B-probe (1 mg mL−1, 50 μL), G-probe (1 mg mL−1, 25 μL) and MFX (0.01 mol L−1, 50 μL). The volume was then adjusted to 5 mL with a solvent mixture of ethanol and distilled water in a 1:1 ratio for incubating 10 min. The FL intensity of system assigned as (F513/F442)0. Then, 0.01 mol L−1 of Cu2+ solution (50 μL) was added, and the FL intensity was collected at 442 nm and 513 nm after incubating 30 min assigned as F513/F442. The temperature and incubating time were optimized, and each parameter was measured three times in parallel.

The milk samples were purchased from local supermarkets and were centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 10 min to remove the fat. To precipitate the protein, 1 mL of methanol was added to 5 mL sample centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered with microporous filter (0.22 μm). The B-probe (1 mg mL−1, 50 μL) and G-probe (1 mg mL−1, 25 μL) were added in samples and the volume was diluted to 5 mL containing MFX (0.3, 0.5 and 0.8 μM) or Cu2+ (0.5, 1.0 and 1.5 μM). The FL intensity was collected at 442 nm and 513 nm.

The properties of the B-probe (N-doped CDs) were characterized by TEM, nanoparticle size analyzer and XPS. Fig. 1 a and 1b showed that the N-doped CDs exhibited a regular morphology and were well-dispersed. As shown in Fig. 1c, the size of N-doped CDs was about 4–6 nm. The XPS spectrum of N-doped CDs was shown in Fig. 1d. The binding energies of C1s, O1s, and N1s were located at 284.65, 531.92 and 399.70 eV, respectively. The atomic content of C, O and N elements was 67.99 %, 20.91 % and 11.1 %. In addition, XPS high resolution spectrum of C1s, O1s, and N1s were shown in ES I Figs. S2a, S2b, S2c. The binding energy at 284.36 eV, 284.95 eV, 285.72 eV and 287.67 eV were owing to C–C, C–O, C–N, and CO, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s can be fitted into two single peaks that correspond to CO (531.81 eV) and C–OH (532.02 eV). The N1s peaks located at 399.49 eV and 400.11 eV were attributed to C–N–C and N–H.

The UV–visible spectroscopy (UV) and fluorescence (FL) spectrum of B-probe, G-probe and the B/G probe were shown in Fig. 1e, 1f, 1g. The B-probe generally exhibits strong absorption in the ultraviolet region and the FL emission peaks located at 442 nm. The B-probe solution exhibited intense blue fluorescence. The absorption range of the G-probe extends up to the visible region and the FL emission peaks are located at 513 nm. The G-probe with strong green fluorescence was observed by exciting at 365 nm. The B/G probe exhibited strong absorption in the ultraviolet and visible region. The results showed that the B/G probe can give out yellow-green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light excitation.

As shown in the FT-IR spectrum of B-probe (Fig. 1h), the absorption peak at 3320 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H bond. The stretching vibration of C–H bond was located at 2976 cm−1. The peak around 1681 cm−1 was the characteristic absorption peak of C=O bond. The absorption peak at 1459 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of C–H bond. These characteristic peaks were consistent with the carbon quantum dots reported in the literature [25]. The broad peak at 3440 cm−1 in the G-probe belonged to the O–H stretching vibration. The vibration peak at 1614 and 1571 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the C=C bonds within the benzene rings. The peak around 1668 cm−1 was the stretching absorption of C=O. The peak located at 1386 cm−1 was attributed to the deformation vibration of –CH3. The peak appearing at 2854 cm−1 and 2769 cm−1 belonged to Fermi resonance between the C–H stretching vibration and frequency doubling of the C–H deformation vibration.

FL spectra of B-probe, G-probe and the B/G probe were shown in Fig. 2 a, 2b, 2c. The FL emission peaks of B-probe at 442 nm did not significantly change when the excitation wavelength ranged from 330 nm to 390 nm. Furthermore, the emission spectra of the G-probe were also found to be independent of the excitation wavelength in the range of 380–500 nm. The FL emission peak of G-probe was 513 nm. When B-probe and G-probe mixed, a dual emission fluorescence probe was formed. The FL spectrum of the resulting B/G probe exhibited two distinct emission peaks at 442 nm and 513 nm. Notably, these FL emission peaks did not shift with an increase in the excitation wavelength. As shown in ESI Fig. S3, the UV curve of the B/G probe had two absorption peaks at 249 nm and 470 nm, which might be originated from the π-π∗ transition in C=C and the n-π∗ transition in C=O. The excitation spectrum and ultraviolet spectrum of the B/G probe overlapped well. In addition, the excitation and emission spectrum of the B-probe and G-probe were shown in ESI Fig. S4 and ESI Fig. S5.

The effects of pH, time, NaCl and solvents on the stability of the B/G probe were systematically investigated. As depicted in Fig. 2d, across a broad pH range from 4 to 10, the FL intensity at 442 nm and 513 nm remained relatively constant, demonstrating that the B/G probe possesses excellent pH stability. As shown in Fig. 2e, the FL intensity of 442 nm and 513 nm remained high and stable over a period of 5 days illustrating that the B/G probe had good time stability. Ionic strength is another factor that has a great influence on fluorescence intensity. As illustrated in Fig. 2f, when the concentration of NaCl increased to 0.5 mol L−1, the FL intensity of the B/G probe was not affected. The results illustrated that the probe could withstand higher ionic strength. The solvents such as acetonitrile, methanol, water, acetone, ethanol-water 1:1, ethanol-water 1:2, ethanol-water 1:3 on the stability of the B/G probe were listed in Fig. 2g. The solvent had a significant impact on the fluorescence of the B/G probe and ethanol-water 1:1 was selected as the optimal solvent.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, when MFX was added to the B/G probe, a significant change occurred in the FL spectrum of the B/G probe. The fluorescence intensity at 513 nm increased significantly, while the fluorescence intensity at 442 nm decreased significantly. However, the FL spectrum of both the B-probe and G-probe exhibited little change before and after the addition of MFX. This suggests that the presence of MFX did not significantly alter the fluorescence properties of these probes (ESI Fig. S6a and Fig. S6b). In addition, as shown in ESI Fig. S7a, Fig. S7b and Fig. S7c, when copper ions were added to B-probe, G-probe and B/G-probe, there was no significant change in fluorescence intensity. When moxifloxacin is not added, copper ions do not elicit a response from the B-probe, G-probe, and B/G-probe. These results also indicate that the B/G-probe exhibits good selectivity for MFX. Influences of pH, time, and temperature on the determination of MFX using the B/G probe were researched systematically. As shown in Fig. 3b, the value of (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0 changed when pH increased from 3 to 10. The maximum value of (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0 was obtained at pH 3. The ionic form of the MFX is changed correspondingly when the pH changed. Moxifloxacin is a zwitterionic molecule with two proton-binding sites, an alkaline group at the 7-position and an acidic group at the 3-position. In aqueous solution, moxifloxacin exists in four microspecies, namely the cationic form (H2Q+), the zwitterionic form (HQ±), the neutral form (HQo), and the anionic form (Q−) [26]. The pK1 and pK2 value of moxifloxacin is (6.25 ± 0.02) and (9.29 ± 0.04) [26,27]. The ionic form may affect the contact between MFX and the probe, thus pH could affect the detection of MFX. At pH 3, the ionic form of the MFX is cationic form (H2Q+) [26]. Therefore, H2Q+ was more likely to interact with the B/G probe. As shown in Fig. 3c and d, the incubation time had little effect on the value of (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0. As the temperature increased, the value of (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0 decreased. Therefore, 20 min and 25 °C were optimum parameters. Under optimal detection conditions, good linear relationship ranges from 0.05 to 0.5 μM (y = 10.05173x+1.46265, R
2 = 0.99253) and range from 0.5 to 0.9 μM (y = 4.50937x+4.16977, R
2 = 0.98485) by the quenching efficiencies (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0 vs the concentrations of MFX was shown in Fig. 3e. The corresponding fluorescence emission spectra induced by various concentrations of moxifloxacin were offered in ESI Fig. S8. The detection limit (DL) can be expressed as 3σ/S, the S was the slope of the calibration curve, σ was the standard deviation of the response (n = 12). DL was obtained to be 17.5 nM for MFX. The quantitation limit (QL) can be expressed as 10σ/S and was obtained to be 58.3 nM for MFX. The relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 6) of the within-day and between-days was 2.6 % and 4.4 %, respectively.

Due to the carboxyl group on the MFX can form complex with Cu2+, the B/G probe-MFX was used as an intermediary to detect Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 4 a, when the Cu2+ was added into the B/G probe-MFX, the FL spectrum of the B/G probe-MFX had significantly changed. The fluorescence intensity at both 513 nm and 442 nm decreased. Cu2+ ions are prone to precipitation under alkaline conditions, hence a pH of 3 was chosen for the experiments. Additionally, the effects of incubation time and temperature on the detection of Cu2+ were examined over a range of 5–30 min and temperatures from 25 to 45 °C, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, the incubation time had little effect on the value of (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0. Therefore, 25 min was selected as optimum incubation time. As shown in Fig. 4c, as the temperature increased, the value of (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0 decreases. The reason was that the stability constants (log K1 and log K2) for the MXF complexes toward Cu2+ increase with increasing temperature [28]. Therefore, 45 °C was selected as optimum temperature. Under optimal detection conditions, As shown in Fig. 4d, a good linearity was observed between (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0 and Cu2+ concentrations of 0.05–1.9 μM (y = −1.17815x+4.70768, R
2 = 0.98646). In addition, the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra induced by various concentrations of Cu2+ were offered in ESI Fig. S9. The DL and QL was 32.5 and 108.8 nM for Cu2+, respectively. The RSD of the within-day and between-days was 2.3 % and 2.7 %, respectively (n = 6).

Selectivity is of paramount importance for the recognition of specific targets. The antibiotics including MFX, florfenicol (FF), thiamphenicol (TAP), sulfamethoxazole (SMX), erythromycin (ERY), tetracycline (TC), chloramphenicol (CAP), levofloxacin (LVX), norfloxacin (NOR), sulfadimidine (SDI), ciprofloxacin (CIP), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), and metal ions including K+、Na+、Ca2+、Mg2+、Al3+、Zn2+、Ni2+、Fe3+、Cu2+、Pb2+、Co2+, small molecules including glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), tryptophane (Try), phenylalanine (Phe), lysine (Lys), tyrosine (Tyr) were used to evaluating the selectivity of the B/G probe. As shown in Fig. 5 a, 5b, and 5c, only the addition of moxifloxacin to the B/G probe resulted in a significant increase in the F513/F442 value, demonstrating that the B/G probe has excellent selectivity for moxifloxacin.

The anti-interference capability is also crucial for detection. The value of (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0 was used to evaluate the anti-interference ability of the B/G probe for the detection of MFX. As shown in Fig. 5d, 5e and 5f, the coexistence of interfering substances had little effect on the value of (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0, except for Cu2+. The reason might be the strong binding affinity of MFX to Cu2+. The results illustrated that the B/G probe exhibited superior anti-interference capabilities, enabling accurate determination of MFX even in the presence of interfering substances. In addition, the anti-interference capability of the B/G probe-MFX as turn-on sensor for Cu2+ determination was discussed similarly. As shown in Fig. 5g, 5h and 5i, the results indicated that the value of (F513/F442)/(F513/F442)0 was not affected by the coexistence of interfering substances. Therefore, the B/G probe had better anti-interference capability for MFX and Cu2+.

The applicability of the B/G probe was evaluated by determining the spiked recoveries of MFX in milk samples. Different concentrations of MFX (0.2, 0.4, 0.6 μM) and Cu2+ (0.5, 1.0, 1.5 μM) were spiked in the milk samples, respectively. As seen in Table 1 , the recoveries of MFX ranged from 95.1 % to 106.7 % with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) no more than 5.0 % (n = 3). The recoveries of Cu2+ ranged from 95.3 % to 104.0 % with RSDs no more than 5.0 % (n = 3). Additionally, HPLC and ICP-AES were employed to confirm the reliability of the results obtained with the B/G probe. A t-test was utilized to assess whether there were any statistically significant differences between the concentrations determined by these two methods. The t-values were derived from the following equation (1).
(1)
t =
|
x ‾
− T
|
s
n
Here
x ‾
, s represents the average value and standard deviation of the concentrations measured by the B/G probe (n = 3); T denotes the concentrations determined by HPLC or ICP-AES. The calculated t-Test values were less than the critical t-value at the 0.05 significance level for 2 degrees of freedom, which is t 0.05,2 = 4.30. This indicates that there is no statistically significant difference between the concentrations determined by the B/G probe and those determined by HPLC or ICP-AES. The above results indicated that this method had good accuracy and good precision for the analysis of MFX and Cu2+. Comparative information with other methods for determination of MFX and Cu2+ were shown in ESI Table S1 and Table S2. This method exhibited the advantages of lower DL. Furthermore, the B/G probe had the merits of better selective and anti-interference capability for MFX and Cu2+. In addition, it is easy to prepare the B/G probe.

The possible mechanism of the interaction between MFX and the B/G probe was explored. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence excitation spectra of the B/G probe partially overlap with the absorption spectra of MFX indicating that the inner-filter effect (IFE) was the important mechanism for the quenching features [29]. The fluorescence emission spectra of the B/G probe did not overlap with the absorption spectra of MFX, ruling out fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [30]. Then, the fluorescence lifetimes of the B/G probe with and without MFX were measured. As shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence lifetime of the B/G probe was 4.14 ns. The fluorescence lifetime of the B/G probe + MFX was 4.19 ns. When MFX was added, the fluorescence lifetime had no change indicating that FRET was excluded [31].

The Zeta potential in Fig. 6c increased from −17.20 to −9.08 mV after adding MFX to the B/G probe, further elucidating the stronger coordination between MFX to the B/G probe. When Cu2+ ions were added in the B/G probe + MFX, the zeta potential further increased to −2.56 mV, indicating that Cu2+ ions have bound to the B/G probe + MFX (The curve of Zeta Ppotential was shown in ESI Fig. S10). Fig. 6d further showed that after adding MFX caused the appearing characteristic peak of MFX in the XPS spectrum. When Cu2+ ions were added in the B/G probe + MFX, the XPS spectrum exhibited characteristic peaks of Cu2+. As illustrated in Fig. 6e, the emission spectra of the B/G probe was barely affected by Cu2+ addition. When Cu2+ ions were added in the B/G probe + MFX, the fluorescence intensity at both 513 nm and 442 nm decreased. These results further proving that MFX first binds to the B/G probe, followed by the binding of Cu2+ to the B/G probe + MFX. As shown in ESI Fig. S11, Cu2+ can strongly bind to the C3-carboxyl and C4-ketone groups of the MFX [32]. Therefore, MFX was used as an intermediary to detect Cu2+.

The static quenching and the dynamic quenching were discussed using the Stern–Volmer equation (eqn (2) ) [33].
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where F0 is steady-state FL intensities of the B/G probe without MFX [F513/F442)0], F is steady-state FL intensities of the B/G probe with MFX (F513/F442), K
sv is Stern–Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of MFX. As shown in E SI Fig. S12, the Stern-Volmer plot was downward curved suggesting the presence of two fluorophore populations with one of them not being accessible to the quencher [34]. The results also showed the coexistence of static and dynamic quenching.

In this work, we constructed a dual emission fluorescence probe with outstanding optical properties and good water dispersion by combining N-doped carbon dots with coumarin derivatives. This dual-emission fluorescence probe demonstrates outstanding selectivity and anti-interference capabilities. The inner-filter effect is identified as the key mechanism behind the quenching characteristics. Moreover, Cu2+ ions can strongly bind to the MFX. This probe has been developed for the sensitive and selective quantitative detection of MFX, followed by Cu2+ ions. The detection limit was 17.5 nM and 32.5 nM for MFX and Cu2+ ions, respectively. A satisfactory recovery rate, ranging from 95.1 % to 106.7 % for MFX and from 95.3 % to 104.0 % for Cu2+, indicated that the B/G probe had good accuracy for the detection of MFX and Cu2+ ions. Furthermore, this dual-emission probe offers a novel approach for subsequent food health and environmental monitoring.

Qihui Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Ju Yang: Data curation. Siqi Yang: Data curation. Jiagen Li: Writing – review & editing. Congdi Chen: Writing – review & editing. Jiexue Wang: Writing – review & editing. Zhihui Xiong: Writing – review & editing. Kang Sun: Writing – review & editing. Guowei Deng: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Sichuan Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 2025ZNSFSC1062), Opening Project of Sichuan Provincial Key Laboratory for Structural Optimization and Application of Functional Molecules (No. GNFZ202406), the Opening Project of Medical Imaging Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province (No. MIKL202301), the Scientific Research Innovation Team Funds of Chengdu Normal University (No. CSCXTD2020A05) and the Young Teacher Support Project of Chengdu Normal University (No. CS23QN04).
